title: Path convergence in diffusion models
authors: Roi Holtzman, Roman Beauvallet, Werner Krauth
abstract: We discuss diffusion-model paths interpolating between a target distribution known only through p patterns and a reference distribution that can be sampled. These interpolating paths can be constructed symmetrically or else in forward direction (often referred to as a "noising") from the target patterns to the reference distribution or in backward direction (as a "denoising") from the reference distribution to the patterns. For backward paths with identical diffusion noise, we consider the path convergence in number of patterns p towards the path for infinitely many patterns. In a one-dimensional test case, we show that this convergence is on a scale 1/sqrt(p), but with infinite mean square deviation. We demonstrate that the path convergence allows for extrapolation towards the p=infinity path which samples the target distribution. We provide a proof-of-concept extrapolation algorithm and propose the convergence and extrapolation of paths as a possible strategy for density estimation and generalization. We illustrate all our algorithms through pseudo-codes and provide Python implementations.
summary: This paper investigates the theoretical concept of path convergence in diffusion models used to bridge a known reference distribution to a target distribution defined only by a finite set of patterns. By analyzing how paths converge as the number of patterns increases, the authors propose and demonstrate an extrapolation algorithm that allows for estimating properties corresponding to an infinite number of samples. This work provides a mathematical framework for generalization in density estimation.
main_problem: The paper analyzes path convergence when constructing interpolating paths between a target distribution (known only via $p$ patterns) and a reference distribution using diffusion model formalism.
main_result: Path convergence is shown to allow for extrapolation towards the infinite pattern limit ($p=\infty$), providing a strategy for density estimation and generalization beyond the available samples.
method: The authors develop algorithms for symmetric, forward, and backward path construction in imaginary time, analyzing how these paths converge as $p$ increases.
model_or_system: The framework uses diffusion models to connect patterns $\{x_{\mu}^0\}$ of a target distribution $\pi_T$ to samples $x_ eta \sim \pi_R$. The analysis is initially performed in the free-particle case using path integrals derived from statistical mechanics.
key_observables: Path convergence scale ($1/\sqrt{p}$), mean square deviation, and the change in distance upon extrapolation ($\langle |	ilde{x}_{p+q}^	au(Y) - x_\infty^	au| angle - \langle |x_{p+q}^	au - x_\infty^	au| angle$).
important_parameters: Number of patterns ($p$), time step ($\Delta	au$), and the limit $p 	o \infty$.
main_assumptions: The analysis focuses on establishing convergence and extrapolation strategies, noting that the core development is a proof-of-concept for generalization.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate path construction (finite vs. small $\Delta	au$), show path convergence by comparing discrete paths ($p$) to continuous limits ($p=\infty$), and demonstrate successful extrapolation using two sets of patterns ($\{p\}$ and $\{q\}$).
paper_structure: The paper systematically introduces the path integral formalism, details three construction algorithms (symmetric, forward, backward), analyzes the convergence behavior in the limit $p 	o \infty$, and concludes with a proof-of-concept extrapolation algorithm.
why_interesting: The mathematical machinery developed for analyzing path convergence and extrapolation directly relates to understanding how physical systems described by limited data can be generalized or extrapolated using modern generative models, which has implications across statistical physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Backward path construction. (a): At finite ∆τ, the distribution of xτ−∆τ starting at xτ is given by a sum of p reweighted Gaussians (in green). Algorithm 3 (backward-construction) samples the pattern µτ as the end- point xµτ 0 of a Gaussian bridge for xτ−∆τ . (b): For small ∆τ, the p Gaussians of (a) merge into a single Gaussian (see Alg. 4).

Fig. 2: Path convergence, velocity fields in our test case for β = 30. (a): Discrete backward paths for differ- ent p under identical random elements (ητ, γτ), from Alg. 3 (backward-construction). Endpoints xµτ 0 are indicated for τ = 6. (b): Continuous backward paths (∆τ = 0.01) from Alg. 4, with velocity field for p = 16. Upper inset: velocity field for p = ∞. Lower inset: Closeup.
